prompt: Name one commonly-praised idea, trend, or practice in modern knowledge work that you think is overrated, and explain why.
response:
One commonly praised idea in modern knowledge work that I think is overrated is the concept of "deep work." The idea, popularized by Cal Newport, is that the key to being highly productive is to engage in focused, distraction-free work for long periods of time. While I agree that being able to concentrate is important, I don't think the extreme version of deep work is as valuable as it's made out to be.

In practice, I think the deep work concept can be taken to unrealistic and unproductive extremes. It's not feasible or healthy for most people to cut themselves off from all distractions and spend hours in uninterrupted focus, day after day. We all have limits to our attention spans and need breaks to recharge. Attempting to constantly work in a deep, focused state can lead to burnout.

Additionally, I believe the idea that deep work is the only path to being a successful knowledge worker is misguided. There are many important tasks and skills that require communication, collaboration, and dealing with distractions. Knowledge work also benefits from taking breaks to let ideas incubate in the background. 

So while I agree that the ability to focus is important, I don't think the deep work philosophy is as critical or widely applicable as some make it out to be. A balanced approach that incorporates both focused work and other activities is probably wiser for most people. The deep work concept can be useful in moderation, but when taken to extremes I believe it is overrated and not the key to success it's often portrayed as.
